1: The theme of Jesus proclaiming the good news of the kingdom from town to town fulfills Isaiah 52:7 (“how beautiful are the feet of those who bring good news”) and Isaiah 61:1 (“the Spirit of the Lord is on me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news” — the text Jesus explicitly claimed at Nazareth, Luke 4:18). Jesus is himself the fulfillment of the Isaianic herald; his itinerant preaching enacts the promised Jubilee-liberation.
2: The women healed of evil spirits and diseases are the first fruits of the liberation Jesus proclaimed at Nazareth (Luke 4:18: “to set the oppressed free”). Mary Magdalene, from whom seven demons were cast out, is a type of the radical deliverance Christ brings — the complete undoing of demonic bondage. The number seven (completeness) signals total possession followed by total liberation, foreshadowing the comprehensive defeat of Satan accomplished through the cross and resurrection (Colossians 2:15).
3: Joanna, Susanna, and many others who supported the ministry from their own resources are part of the new community gathering around Jesus. The theme of women providing for the work of God recalls those who gave generously for the Tabernacle (Exodus 35:25–26) and the Shunammite woman who supported Elisha (2 Kings 4:8–10). The women’s voluntary, self-funded participation in the mission anticipates the Spirit-led generosity of the early church (Acts 2:44–45; Luke 4:34–35).
4: The large crowd gathering and people coming from every town creates the context for the Sower parable. Jesus as the one who speaks to the crowd in parables fulfills Psalms 78:2 (“I will open my mouth in parables, I will utter hidden things, things from of old”), cited in Matthew 13:35 as a direct prophetic fulfillment. The gathering of the crowd itself images the eschatological ingathering of the nations around the Word of God (Isaiah 2:2–3; Micah 4:1–2).
5: The Sower going out to sow is a type: Jesus is the divine Sower of Isaiah 55:10–11 (“as the rain and snow come down from heaven… so is my word that goes out from my mouth: it will not return to me empty, but will accomplish what I desire”). The seed on the path, trampled and devoured, anticipates the rejection of Jesus’s word by those who hear without receiving — and ultimately the rejection of Jesus himself by those whose hearts the evil one has hardened.
6: The rocky ground where the seed sprouts quickly and withers for lack of moisture is a theme of rootless, transient faith that cannot sustain itself under pressure. The imagery draws on Jeremiah 17:8 (the person who trusts in the LORD is like a tree by water with deep roots that does not fear drought, contrasted with the shrub in the desert). Christ is the one who makes deep roots possible; union with him is what transforms the rocky soil into well-watered ground (John 15:5).
7: The thorns that choke the good seed are “life’s worries, riches and pleasures” (Luke 8:14) — the competing loyalties that prevent the word from maturing. The thorn-and-briar imagery evokes the cursed ground of Genesis 3:17–18; the world that produces thorns rather than fruit is the fallen order. Christ’s crown of thorns at the passion (John 19:2) bears the curse of the thorn-producing ground, transforming what chokes life into the instrument of his sacrificial death for life.
8: The hundredfold yield on the good soil is the revelation of the kingdom’s disproportionate fruitfulness — echoing Isaac’s hundredfold harvest as a sign of covenant blessing (Genesis 26:12). The final declaration “Whoever has ears to hear, let them hear” is a direct invitation to be the good soil. Christ as both Sower and Word (Luke 8:11 identifies the seed with the word of God; John 1:1 identifies the Word with Christ) sows himself into the world to produce a harvest that exceeds all natural expectation.
9: The disciples’ private question (“what does this parable mean?”) marks the beginning of Jesus’s interpretive teaching for those who have been given the secrets of the kingdom. The distinction between inside and outside mirrors Isaiah 6:9–10’s “ever hearing but never understanding” — the paradox of revelation that simultaneously discloses and conceals. Jesus as the revealer of secrets fulfills the role of the one in Daniel 2:28 who “reveals mysteries” and Colossians 1:26–27 (the mystery now disclosed = Christ in you).
10: This is a near-direct citation of Isaiah 6:9–10 (“seeing they may not see; and hearing they may not understand”). The passage is cited across the NT as a fulfillment of Israel’s hardening in response to Jesus’s proclamation (Matthew 13:14–15; John 12:40; Acts 28:26–27; Romans 11:8). The secrets (mystēria) of the kingdom given to the disciples are what Paul identifies as “the mystery hidden for long ages past, but now revealed” (Romans 16:25–26) — Christ himself as the content of the mystery.
11: “The seed is the word of God” — a direct Christological identification: John 1:1 identifies Jesus as the Word of God; Hebrews 4:12 describes the word of God as living and active; 1 Peter 1:23–25 identifies the imperishable seed with the living and enduring word. Jesus is simultaneously the Sower (the one who proclaims) and the Seed (the word proclaimed): both the agent and the content of the kingdom proclamation. This dual identification grounds the Sower parable’s Christological depth.
12: The devil taking away the word from the heart echoes the cosmic conflict inaugurated in Gen 3 (the serpent taking God’s word from Eve through deception). Satan’s strategy throughout redemptive history is to prevent the word from taking root. Christ’s coming “to destroy the devil’s work” (1 John 3:8) is the counter-strategy: his death and resurrection definitively defeats the one who has the power of death (Hebrews 2:14–15) and reclaims hearts for the Word.
13: The rocky ground receiving the word with joy but having no root describes superficial faith that does not survive temptation. The theme of rootedness in Christ is explicit in Colossians 2:6–7 (“just as you received Christ Jesus as Lord, continue to live your lives in him, rooted and built up in him”). The one who endures to the end is saved (Matthew 24:13); the root that holds is union with Christ, who is himself the vine from which all fruitful branches draw their life (John 15:4–5).
14: The thorny-soil hearer choked by worries, riches, and pleasures represents the divided heart — the inability to serve two masters (Luke 16:13). The fruit-prevention is not dramatic apostasy but quiet suffocation: the word is heard, but the competing loyalties slowly displace it. Jeremiah’s new covenant promise (Luke 31:33) addresses this: the law written on the heart by the Spirit replaces the divided, half-responsive heart. Christ’s Spirit is the agent of the transformation from thorny to good soil.
15: “Those with a noble and good heart, who hear the word, retain it, and by persevering produce a crop” — the good soil is the new covenant community described by Ezekiel 36:26–27 (“I will give you a new heart… I will put my Spirit in you and move you to follow my decrees”). The noble and good heart is not a natural quality but a gift; the fruit produced is the fruit of the Spirit (Galatians 5:22–23). The whole progression of the parable (sowing → growth → harvest) images the redemptive work of Christ from proclamation to final harvest.
16: The lamp on a stand is a theme pointing to Christ as the light of the world (John 8:12; Luke 9:5). No one lights a lamp to conceal it — the entire purpose of revelation is disclosure. Jesus as the fulfillment of the lamp imagery: the light given at creation (Genesis 1:3) that Isaiah 60:1–3 promises will shine through the glory of YHWH rising upon Israel is now present in Jesus (2 Corinthians 4:6: “the light of the knowledge of God’s glory displayed in the face of Christ”). The church is the lampstand that holds this light before the world (Revelation 1:20; Matthew 5:14–16).
17: “Nothing hidden that will not be disclosed” — the hiddenness of Christ’s identity during the earthly ministry (the messianic secret) is temporary; the resurrection and parousia are the full disclosure. The theme of revealed mystery pervades Paul’s letters: the mystery hidden for ages is now disclosed (Ephesians 3:4–6; Colossians 1:26–27). Everything about Jesus that was concealed in his earthly life — his divine identity, his substitutionary death, his coming glorification — will be fully brought into the open.
18: “Whoever has will be given more; whoever does not have, even what they think they have will be taken from them” — the eschatological principle of compounding: those who receive the word and respond grow in understanding; those who refuse the word lose even the residual understanding they possess. The theme of increasing revelation to those who follow Christ and decreasing hardening for those who reject him runs from Isaiah 6:9–10 through the Synoptic parables to Rom 9–11. Listening carefully to Christ is the condition for increasing participation in the mystery he reveals.
19: Jesus’s mother and brothers coming to see him sets up the redefinition of family in Luke 8:21. The theme of Christ transcending biological family reflects his unique relationship to the Father: his true family is those who do the Father’s will (Matthew 12:50). This also anticipates the new family created by the Spirit in the church (Galatians 3:26–28: “you are all children of God through faith”; Ephesians 2:19: “members of God’s household”). The inability to reach Jesus physically (the crowd) contrasts with the access of those who hear God’s word.
20: The report “your mother and brothers are standing outside, wanting to see you” presents Jesus with a claim on his attention based on biological kinship. In the OT, family solidarity (mishpachah) was the primary social bond; Jesus’s response will redraw the boundaries of the covenant family around hearing and doing God’s word rather than physical descent. This redefinition is part of the new covenant: membership in God’s household is by hearing and faith, not by birth (John 1:12–13).
21: “My mother and brothers are those who hear God’s word and put it into practice” — Jesus defines his true family as the hearing-and-doing community. This is a direct fulfillment of Jeremiah 31:33 (the new covenant: God’s law written on hearts, everyone knowing the LORD) and Ezekiel 36:27 (the Spirit moving God’s people to obey his decrees). The community gathered around Christ’s word is the new household of God (Ephesians 2:19; 1 Timothy 3:15), constituted not by blood but by the Spirit.
22: The disciples entering the boat at Jesus’s initiative (“let’s go over to the other side”) is the setting for the storm-stilling. The lake crossing carries the biblical charge of the Reed Sea crossing (Exod 14) — the original divine passage through dangerous water. Christ leads his disciples through the waters, as Moses led Israel; but here the water is subject to Christ’s command, not separated by his rod. The lake as a setting for Jesus’s sovereign action anticipates his walking on water (absent in Luke but present in Matt 14; Mark 6).
23: Jesus falling asleep in the storm is the image of serene, sovereign rest in the midst of chaos — echoing Psalms 4:8 (“in peace I will lie down and sleep”) and Jonah 1:5 (Jonah sleeping through the storm). The Jonah parallel is explicit in Matthew 12:40 (“as Jonah was three days in the belly of the fish, so the Son of Man will be three days in the heart of the earth”); Luke’s storm narrative echoes the Jonah scene without direct citation. Christ’s sleeping in the storm reveals his authority as the one who holds all storms in his hand.
24: “He got up and rebuked the wind and the raging water; the storm subsided, and all was calm” — the direct parallel to Psalms 107:29 (“he stilled the storm to a whisper; the waves of the sea were hushed”) and Psalms 89:9 (“you rule over the surging sea; when its waves mount up, you still them”). The rebuke of the wind and waves is the divine creative authority of Psalms 104:7 (“at your rebuke the waters fled”). Jesus performs what Psalms 107:23–32 attributes to YHWH alone: saving those in peril at sea by commanding the storm to cease.
25: “Who is this? He commands even the winds and the water, and they obey him” — the disciples’ question is the central Christological question of the Gospel. The answer the narrative supplies is implicit: only YHWH commands the sea (Job 38:8–11; Psalms 65:7; Luke 89:9). Jesus’s question “where is your faith?” assumes that their faith should have been in him as the one with divine authority over creation. The storm-stilling is the central revelation of God in Luke 8: God in Christ is the Lord of creation, present with his people in their peril.
26: The region of the Gerasenes across the lake from Galilee is Gentile territory; the lake crossing brings Jesus to the pagan side of the Jewish world. The geographical crossing from Jewish Galilee to Gentile Decapolis is a type of the Gentile mission that the Gospel anticipates throughout — the God of Israel crossing the boundary to rescue those outside the covenant. The healed demoniac of Luke 8:39 will become the first Gentile missionary, anticipating the Acts narrative of the gospel’s spread to the nations.
27: The demon-possessed man living among the tombs, naked, uncontrollable, outside the city — is a vivid portrait of humanity under total demonic domination: excluded from community, stripped of clothing (dignity), dwelling among the dead. His condition is the extreme of what the parable of the Prodigal Son describes (Luke 15:15–16): existence at the furthest remove from the Father’s house. Christ crossing the sea to reach this man demonstrates the initiative of the incarnation itself — the Son coming to find the utterly lost (Luke 19:10).
28: The demon’s recognition of Jesus — “What do you want with me, Jesus, Son of the Most High God? I beg you, don’t torture me!” — is the supernatural knowledge of the defeated acknowledging the victor. The title “Son of the Most High God” is the same used by the Gentile girl in Acts 16:17 (“These men are servants of the Most High God”) — the universal divine title. The demons’ acknowledgment of Jesus’s identity is the forced confession anticipating the universal acknowledgment of Philippians 2:10–11: every knee will bow.
29: The demonic power that had repeatedly broken chains and driven the man into the wilderness is the type of the bondage from which Christ comes to free humanity. The chains and guards unable to hold the demoniac image the futility of human attempts to manage the problem of spiritual bondage without divine intervention. Isaiah 49:24–25 asks: “Can the prey be taken from the mighty man?” and answers: “But this is what the LORD says: Yes, captives will be taken from warriors, and plunder retrieved from the fierce.” Christ is the fulfillment of this promise.
30: “Legion” — the name of the many demons inhabiting the man — echoes the Roman military occupation (a Legion was approximately 6,000 soldiers); the name may carry both the literal sense (many demons) and a political-symbolic resonance. Christ’s authority over Legion demonstrates his authority over every power that oppresses humanity — spiritual and political. Colossians 2:15 describes the cross as the disarming of the powers and authorities, making a public spectacle of them: the Gerasene exorcism is a type of this cosmic disarmament.
31: The demons’ begging not to be sent into the Abyss (abyssos) reveals their awareness of Christ’s eschatological authority. The Abyss (the bottomless pit of Revelation 9:1–2; Luke 20:1–3) is the place of the final confinement of demonic powers; Christ’s authority to consign them there is anticipated in his earthly ministry and accomplished definitively at the cross and resurrection (Revelation 20:2–3: Satan bound and thrown into the Abyss). The demons’ fear of the Abyss is the fear of their ultimate defeat.
32: The swine and their death in the lake: the entering of the demons into the unclean animals and their destruction images the judgment of the unclean order. The sea into which the pigs rush is the place of chaos and judgment; the destruction of the swine is a partial anticipation of the demons’ ultimate consignment to the lake of fire (Revelation 20:10). The unclean animals entering the chaotic water and perishing recalls the destruction of Pharaoh’s army in the Red Sea — the judgment of the oppressive power in the waters of death.
33: The entire herd rushing down the steep bank and drowning images the annihilation of the demonic forces — the destruction that the Gerasene man himself had been subjected to is now visited on the demons through the animals they inhabited. The same death the demons had visited on the man (exclusion, bondage, dwelling among the dead) is now their own fate. Christ accomplishes the reversal: what was done to the oppressed is done to the oppressor. This is the pattern of the cross itself — death swallowing Death (1 Corinthians 15:54–55; Hosea 13:14).
34: The pig-tenders running to report “what had happened” to the town are the first witnesses of Jesus’s power in Gentile territory. Their report — like the shepherds’ report at the nativity (Luke 2:17–18) — spreads the news of a transforming divine act beyond the immediate scene. The theme of witness-bearing to what Jesus has done is the foundation of the apostolic proclamation: the disciples and those who have been healed are the testimony to the living God’s intervention (Acts 1:8).
35: “They found the man from whom the demons had gone out, sitting at Jesus’ feet, dressed and in his right mind” — the restored image of God in the healed man. Seated at Jesus’s feet in right mind (sōphronounta, self-controlled, sober-minded) is the opposite of the demonic frenzy that had characterized him. This is the new creation image: the man reconstituted in his humanity at the feet of Christ, the one who restores the image of God (Colossians 3:10; Ephesians 4:24). The fear of the crowd mirrors Israel’s fear at Sinai — they were in the presence of the holy.
36: “Those who had seen it told the people how the demon-possessed man had been cured (esōthē, saved)” — the healing is described with the salvation-word sōzō. The eyewitness testimony establishes the reality of the transformation; the Gerasene exorcism is not reported as inner experience but as publicly verifiable change. The NT pattern of proclamation rests on this same logic: we proclaim what we have seen and heard (1 John 1:1–3). Christ’s saving acts are historical, testifiable, and transformative.
37: The region’s request for Jesus to leave — driven by fear rather than rejection — echoes the pattern of the rejected prophet: Elijah driven from Israel (1 Kings 19:2), Isaiah’s message refused (Isaiah 53:3). Jesus’s peaceful compliance (he left) is itself part of his messianic pattern: he does not impose his presence but offers it and withdraws when refused. The judgment on those who reject him is not immediate punishment but the absence of the one who carries healing — the withdrawal of the Kingdom’s presence.
38: The healed man’s begging to go with Jesus reflects the natural desire of those whom Christ has delivered — to follow him wherever he goes (Luke 9:57). Jesus’s instruction to return home and testify is the commission given before the formal commissioning of the Twelve (Luke 9:1–6): the healed man becomes an informal apostle to the Gentile Decapolis. The pattern anticipates Acts: those delivered by Christ are sent to testify to their own community first.
39: “Return home and tell how much God has done for you” — and the man told “how much Jesus had done for him”: the implicit equation of Jesus with God. The man’s free indirect speech (Jesus said “God,” the man said “Jesus”) is Luke’s quiet Christological identification: what Jesus does is what God does; the man correctly identifies his healer as God acting in Jesus. This is the Johannine pattern (Luke 10:30: “I and the Father are one”) expressed in narrative form.
40: “A crowd welcomed him, for they were all expecting him” — the reception back on the Jewish side of the lake contrasts with the Gerasene request for him to leave. The expectation (prosedokōn, they were waiting for him) is both the natural expectation of those who had seen his previous ministry and the deeper eschatological expectation of Israel waiting for the Lord (Isaiah 40:31; Psalms 130:5–6). Christ’s return to the welcoming crowd images his eschatological return — expected, welcomed by those who have been watching.
41: Jairus, a synagogue leader, falling at Jesus’s feet represents the official Jewish leadership acknowledging Jesus’s authority — a relatively rare occurrence. The posture of prostration is the appropriate response to the presence of divine power (cf. Moses before the burning bush, Exodus 3:5; Joshua before the commander of the LORD’s army, Joshua 5:14). The desperate love of a father for his dying child drives the official past his social reservations — a theme of the divine Father’s love for his dying creation (John 3:16).
42: The only daughter about twelve years old who was dying — monogenēs (only-born, unique), the same word used for the Nain widow’s son (Luke 7:12) and for the boy with the unclean spirit (Luke 9:38), and by John for Jesus himself (John 1:14,18; Luke 3:16). The word is a deliberate Christological marker: Christ, the Father’s only-begotten, died so that the Father’s only-beloved children could be raised. The twelve-year-old girl and the woman who bled twelve years (Luke 8:43) bracket a double restoration of the “daughter of Israel.”
43: The woman with twelve years of hemorrhage is ritually unclean for the entire duration (Leviticus 15:25–27) — twelve years of exclusion from Temple worship, from community meals, from physical contact with others. Her condition is the image of Israel’s long-term uncleanness and exclusion from God’s full presence. Twelve years (the number of the tribes) frames her as a daughter of Israel, representing the whole people’s condition of defilement and need of cleansing — the cleansing that Ezekiel 36:25 promises (“I will sprinkle clean water on you, and you will be clean”).
44: “She touched the edge of his cloak, and immediately her bleeding stopped” — Malachi 4:2 promises: “the sun of righteousness will rise with healing in its wings [kanaph, also: hem/edge of a garment]”. The LXX uses pteryx for the hem/wing, but the Hebrew kanaph deliberately connects the healing-hem of the Messianic Sun with the fringe of the prayer-shawl (Numbers 15:38–40). Those who touched the hem of Jesus’s garment were healed (Matthew 14:36; Mark 6:56) — the Malachi prophecy finding its fulfillment in the touch of Christ’s garment-edge.
45: “Who touched me?” — Jesus’s awareness that power had gone out from him demonstrates his active, conscious exercise of healing power, not passive transmission. The disciples’ confusion (the whole crowd is pressing around you) underscores that Jesus’s perception of the healing touch was supernatural knowledge. The public identification he seeks is for the woman’s benefit: she must be publicly acknowledged, not merely healed in secret. Christ’s healing is always toward full restoration — physical and social.
46: “I know that power has gone out from me” (dynamis) — the same word used for the Spirit’s power at the Annunciation (Luke 1:35) and for the power Jesus conferred on the Twelve (Luke 9:1). The self-aware expenditure of divine power in a healing act reveals the kenotic pattern of the incarnation: Christ gives himself for the healing of others, a self-expenditure that reaches its fullest expression at the cross, where he gives his life as a ransom for many (Mark 10:45).
47: The woman’s trembling declaration “in the presence of all the people” of why she touched him and how she was instantly healed is the public testimony that transforms a private healing into a communal witness. The fear (trembling) before Jesus mirrors the fear appropriate to divine presence (Luke 5:8–9; Luke 8:25; Luke 8:35). Her full declaration — both the act and the healing — is the pattern of the public confession of faith that the NT consistently requires (Romans 10:9–10: belief in the heart and confession with the mouth).
48: “Daughter, your faith has saved you (sesōken). Go in peace” — the salvation-word and the shalom-blessing together complete the restoration. Jesus addresses her as “Daughter” (thygater) — restoring her as a daughter of Abraham (13:16 uses this exact phrase for another healed woman), a full member of the covenant community. The faith that saves is not belief in a principle but trust in the person of Jesus; the peace he pronounces is the shalom of full reconciliation — with God, with community, with self.
49: “Your daughter is dead; don’t bother the teacher anymore” — the voice of despair and finality confronting Jesus’s way. Death is the ultimate human boundary that the messenger assumes cannot be crossed. The message “don’t bother the teacher anymore” is the logic of hopelessness in the face of death — the same logic that crucifixion was designed to communicate: this man is finished, done, dead. Jesus’s response to the death-messenger is the response of the resurrection: death does not set the final boundary.
50: “Don’t be afraid; just believe, and she will be healed (sōthēsetai, saved)” — the salvation-word applied to resurrection from death. Jesus’s instruction to Jairus (fear → faith) is the eschatological instruction for every believer facing death: the resurrection of the dead is guaranteed by Christ’s own resurrection (1 Corinthians 15:20: “Christ has indeed been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep”). The “just believe” in the face of death is the defining posture of resurrection faith.
51: The restriction to Peter, John, James, and the girl’s parents echoes the pattern of Moses on Sinai (only Moses and the elders ascended) and the Transfiguration (only the three on the mountain). The intimate restriction preserves the mystery of the resurrection-sign from premature public disclosure — part of the Lukan pattern of Jesus controlling the revelation of his messianic identity. The resurrection of the girl is given to a restricted circle; the resurrection of Jesus will be proclaimed to the world.
52: “She is not dead but asleep” — the redefinition of death from the perspective of resurrection. Jesus uses the sleep metaphor that Paul will employ in 1 Thessalonians 4:13–14 (“we do not want you to be uninformed about those who sleep in death”) and John 11:11–14 (“our friend Lazarus has fallen asleep; but I am going there to wake him up”). For those in Christ, physical death is sleep from which awakening is certain; the mourners’ laughter exposes the gap between the secular view of death’s finality and Christ’s authority over it.
53: “They laughed at him, knowing that she was dead” — the cynical certainty of death’s finality confronting the resurrection claim. The laughter parallels the incredulity that met the resurrection proclamation (Acts 17:32: “when they heard about the resurrection of the dead, some of them sneered”). The crowd that laughs at Christ’s redefinition of death is excluded from witnessing the miracle; the restricted circle of faith (Luke 8:51) alone is present for the resurrection-sign. Faith opens to resurrection; unbelief remains outside.
54: “My child, get up!” (hē pais, egeire) — the resurrection command. Egeirō (to raise, to get up) is the same verb used for the resurrection of Jesus throughout the NT (Matthew 28:6; Romans 4:25; 1 Corinthians 15:4). The verbal parallel between Jesus raising the girl and Jesus’s own resurrection is deliberate: the same authority that commands the dead girl to rise is the authority at work in the Father raising the Son (John 10:17–18; Luke 11:25). The short, direct command — no prayer, no labored effort, just authoritative speech — reveals the one who is himself the resurrection and the life (John 11:25).
55: “Her spirit returned, and at once she stood up” — the pneuma (spirit/life-breath) returning at the resurrection command echoes Genesis 2:7 (God breathing life into Adam) and Ezekiel 37:5,10 (the breath entering the dry bones). Christ as the one who restores the spirit/breath is the eschatological fulfillment of the divine life-giving that began at creation. The instruction to give her something to eat demonstrates the reality of the bodily resurrection — this is not a ghost but a genuinely revived person — anticipating Jesus’s own eating after his resurrection (Luke 24:41–43).
56: The parents’ astonishment and Jesus’s instruction not to tell anyone complete the mystery pattern of the chapter: the resurrection-sign is real but not yet for public proclamation. The silence Jesus commands is the silence of the messianic mystery not yet ready to be fully revealed — the full meaning of “Christ and him crucified” (1 Corinthians 2:2) awaits the cross and resurrection to be understood. The whole chapter (word-sowing, storm-stilling, demonic deliverance, double-healing and resurrection) is a portrait of Christ’s comprehensive sovereignty: over the word, nature, the demonic, disease, and death.
